title: Anastomotic Leak
oneLiner: Surgical emergency – defect at bowel/suture line causing intra-abdominal contamination; high morbidity/mortality if missed.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic shock** – signs: hypotension (MAP <65), tachycardia >110 despite fluid, rising lactate >4 mmol/L. *This is not just a “temperature spike”.*
- **Fulminant peritonitis** – rigidity, board-like abdomen, rebound tenderness, absent bowel sounds. *If present, do not wait for CT – escalate immediately.*
- **Massive haemorrhage from eroded vessel** – sudden fall in Hb, profound hypotension, frank blood per drain or PR. *Suspect if drain output turns fresh blood.*
sections.Presentation:
- Classic onset: post-operative day 3–7 (peak), but can occur up to 2 weeks.
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Abdominal pain (often first sign) – may be disproportionate to exam.
  - Fever (low-grade → high-spiking, rigors).
  - Tachycardia (out of proportion to fever/hypovolaemia).
  - Nausea/vomiting, ileus (failure to pass flatus/stool).
  - Drain output change – serous → purulent, faeculent, or increased volume.
  - **“Unexplained”** new organ failure (oliguria, confusion, rising Cr) – think leak.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Abdominal tenderness (localised → generalised peritonitis).
  - Guarding / rigidity if peritoneal soiling.
  - Wound erythema, purulent discharge.
  - Reduced urine output, prolonged capillary refill.
  - *In early leak: signs may be subtle – isolate a consistently raised heart rate.*
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Paralytic ileus** – no peritonitis, mild distension, usually resolves with NG decompression. *Key differentiator: absence of fever/tachycardia + normal inflammatory markers.*
- **Intra-abdominal abscess (without leak)** – more localised signs, CT shows collection separate from anastomosis. *Splitter: drain output – faeculent or bilious strongly suggests leak.*
- **Pancreatitis (post-op)** – high lipase >3x normal, often epigastric pain radiating to back.
- **Wound infection (superficial)** – no systemic upset, local cellulitis.
- **Pulmonary embolism** – pleuritic chest pain, sudden dyspnoea, Wells score >4.
- **Myocardial injury / ACS** – especially in elderly; troponin rise, ECG changes.
- **Clostridium difficile colitis** – diarrhoea with current/prior antibiotics, toxin positive.

**Splitter:** Single most useful bedside feature = **drain fluid** – biochemical (amylase, bilirubin) or visual: faeculent/bilious = leak until proven otherwise.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Full set of observations & NEWS2.
  - Urine output measurement (catheter if not already).
  - Check drain records – volume, colour, consistency.
  - Test drain fluid with bedside **amylase** stick or lab send for amylase, bilirubin (if upper GI).
  - Point-of-care lactate (arterial/venous).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, Cr, LFTs (including bilirubin, ALP), CRP, **lactate**, blood cultures (x2 sets), coagulation, crossmatch if suspect haemorrhage.
  - *Serial lactates every 4–6 hours to track resuscitation.*
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT abdomen/pelvis **with IV contrast** (and PO/PR water-soluble contrast if tolerated) – sensitivity >90% for leak. *Do not withhold for oral contrast if NBM – IV is essential.*
  - **Second-line:** Water-soluble contrast enema (for low rectal/colonic leaks) – but CT is faster and more comprehensive.
  - **Limited utility:** Abdominal XR – looking for free air (but post-op air is common); may show extraluminal contrast in selected cases.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Drain fluid biochemistry**: amylase >3x serum suggests upper GI leak; bilirubin > serum suggests biliary leak.
  - Diagnostic aspirate / peritoneal tap (if CT equivocal and high suspicion – rarely done, usually surgical decision).
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **No single validated scoring system for leak.** Clinical judgement + elevated CRP + tachycardia (E.g. **CRP >150 mg/L** on POD 3–5 has high sensitivity for leak – but not specific).
- **Never-miss test:** **CT abdomen with IV contrast.** Delay or omission is a serious error – if stable enough, get it. If unstable, escalate before scanning.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ward/ED):**
  - **ABC + sepsis resuscitation** – high-flow O₂, IV access (wide bore), fluid bolus (Hartmann’s 500 mL stat, repeat as per BP/HR/lactate).
  - **Call surgical reg immediately** – do not wait for all results.
  - **NBM** – stop all oral/NG feeds.
  - **NG tube** if not already in situ – free drainage, low suction.
  - **Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics** (see Drugs section) – give first dose within 1 hour of suspicion.
  - **Blood cultures** before antibiotics (if delay minimal).
  - **Analgesia** – morphine IV (titrate) but avoid masking peritonism.
  - **Consider ICU referral** if NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >4, requiring vasopressors.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Surgery** – definitive management is re-laparotomy (or percutaneous drainage for contained abscess without free leak). *Do not delay for trial of medical therapy.*
  - **If not for surgery (e.g. poor candidate)**: percutaneous drainage of collections + antibiotics + supportive care (ICU).
  - **Monitor** – hourly observations (NEWS2), strict fluid balance, urine output, drain output.
  - **Organ support** – may need vasopressors (noradrenaline, under ICU guidance), renal replacement therapy.
  - **Nutrition** – parenteral nutrition (TPN) typically required post-operatively because bowel rest imperative.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Stoma formation often required at re-operation; may be temporary or permanent.
  - Wound care, VTE prophylaxis, physiotherapy.
  - Follow-up surgical clinic 4–6 weeks post-discharge; consider CT or contrast study before stoma reversal.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** Hartmann’s at maintenance + replacement (guided by UOP, losses). Avoid KCl until renal function known.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (check weight, CrCl). *Hold if active bleeding.*
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM completely. NG free drainage.
  - **Observations:** Minimum 4-hourly (NEWS2), more frequently if unstable: 1-hourly for first 24h.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line antibiotics:**
  - **Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV TDS** (check local policy for dose interval if renal impairment).
  - or **Cefuroxime 1.5 g IV TDS + Metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS** as alternative.
- **If penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis):**
  - **Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV BD** (avoid if high risk of MRSA resistance) + Metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS.
  - or **Gentamicin 5–7 mg/kg IV once daily** (check trough levels before second dose) + Metronidazole.
- **Analgesia:**
  - Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV PRN (titrate) or PCA if appropriate.
  - Paracetamol 1g IV QDS (prescribe regularly).
- **Fluid therapy:**
  - Hartmann’s at 1–2 mL/kg/hour maintenance. Boluses as per haemodynamics.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid NSAIDs (risk of acute kidney injury / bleed).
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: caution if CrCl <20; dose adjust.
  - Gentamicin: nephrotoxic, ototoxic; check levels – **trough <1 mg/L** (or <0.5 for once daily); avoid with other nephrotoxins.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Gentamicin** – not checking levels before second dose can cause permanent nephrotoxicity. **Never repeat without trough level.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Septic shock, multi-organ failure.
  - Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS).
  - Acute kidney injury (common, multifactorial).
  - Wound dehiscence / evisceration.
  - Enterocutaneous fistula (chronic, from persistent leak).
  - Intra-abdominal abscess (residual).
- **Chronic:**
  - Prolonged ileus, failure to thrive.
  - Stoma-related issues (if formed).
  - Incisional hernia.
  - Chronic pain / adhesive small bowel obstruction.
sections.Prognosis:
- 30-day mortality ~10–25% (up to 50% if delayed recognition).
- Recovery may require weeks in ICU; many survive with stoma, prolonged TPN.
- Early recognition (within 12–24h of onset) dramatically improves survival.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always review drain fluid colour every shift** – faeculent or bilious is a red flag.
- **Check CRP trajectory daily** – a rise on POD 3–4 is more specific than absolute value.
- **Instruct nurses to test drain fluid with bedside amylase strip** – if positive, escalate rapidly.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not attribute tachycardia to “post-op pain”** – it is a cardinal sign of leak until proven otherwise.
- **Do not stop antibiotics after 24h if no positive cultures** – treat empirically for full course until leak is controlled.
- **Do not wait for “classic” peritonitis** – in early leak, signs may be absent; go with gut feeling + labs.
- **Do not delay CT for oral contrast** – IV contrast is the priority; oral may slow the scan.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** abdominal pain worsens, fever >38°C, vomiting, inability to pass stool/flatus, drain changes colour or starts draining bloody/faeculent fluid.
- **Follow-up:** Surgical clinic in 2 weeks. Advise to contact hospital if any concern (provide dedicated ward or on-call number).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call surgical reg immediately if:** suspicion of leak (NEWS2 ≥5, any sign of peritonitis, haemodynamic instability, lactate >2 mmol/L rising).
- **Call ICU if:** lactate >4 mmol/L, persistent hypotension after 2 L fluid (MAP <65), NEWS2 ≥7, requiring vasopressors, oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/h despite adequate filling.
- **Call crash team if:** unresponsive, peri-arrest (e.g. GCS <8, respiratory rate <8 or >30, systolic BP <90 unresponsive to fluids).

rawMarkdown:
## Anastomotic Leak
**One-liner:** Surgical emergency – defect at bowel/suture line causing intra-abdominal contamination; high morbidity/mortality if missed.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic shock** – signs: hypotension (MAP <65), tachycardia >110 despite fluid, rising lactate >4 mmol/L. *This is not just a “temperature spike”.*
- **Fulminant peritonitis** – rigidity, board-like abdomen, rebound tenderness, absent bowel sounds. *If present, do not wait for CT – escalate immediately.*
- **Massive haemorrhage from eroded vessel** – sudden fall in Hb, profound hypotension, frank blood per drain or PR. *Suspect if drain output turns fresh blood.*

### Presentation
- Classic onset: post-operative day 3–7 (peak), but can occur up to 2 weeks.
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Abdominal pain (often first sign) – may be disproportionate to exam.
  - Fever (low-grade → high-spiking, rigors).
  - Tachycardia (out of proportion to fever/hypovolaemia).
  - Nausea/vomiting, ileus (failure to pass flatus/stool).
  - Drain output change – serous → purulent, faeculent, or increased volume.
  - **“Unexplained”** new organ failure (oliguria, confusion, rising Cr) – think leak.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Abdominal tenderness (localised → generalised peritonitis).
  - Guarding / rigidity if peritoneal soiling.
  - Wound erythema, purulent discharge.
  - Reduced urine output, prolonged capillary refill.
  - *In early leak: signs may be subtle – isolate a consistently raised heart rate.*

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Paralytic ileus** – no peritonitis, mild distension, usually resolves with NG decompression. *Key differentiator: absence of fever/tachycardia + normal inflammatory markers.*
- **Intra-abdominal abscess (without leak)** – more localised signs, CT shows collection separate from anastomosis. *Splitter: drain output – faeculent or bilious strongly suggests leak.*
- **Pancreatitis (post-op)** – high lipase >3x normal, often epigastric pain radiating to back.
- **Wound infection (superficial)** – no systemic upset, local cellulitis.
- **Pulmonary embolism** – pleuritic chest pain, sudden dyspnoea, Wells score >4.
- **Myocardial injury / ACS** – especially in elderly; troponin rise, ECG changes.
- **Clostridium difficile colitis** – diarrhoea with current/prior antibiotics, toxin positive.

**Splitter:** Single most useful bedside feature = **drain fluid** – biochemical (amylase, bilirubin) or visual: faeculent/bilious = leak until proven otherwise.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Full set of observations & NEWS2.
  - Urine output measurement (catheter if not already).
  - Check drain records – volume, colour, consistency.
  - Test drain fluid with bedside **amylase** stick or lab send for amylase, bilirubin (if upper GI).
  - Point-of-care lactate (arterial/venous).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, Cr, LFTs (including bilirubin, ALP), CRP, **lactate**, blood cultures (x2 sets), coagulation, crossmatch if suspect haemorrhage.
  - *Serial lactates every 4–6 hours to track resuscitation.*
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT abdomen/pelvis **with IV contrast** (and PO/PR water-soluble contrast if tolerated) – sensitivity >90% for leak. *Do not withhold for oral contrast if NBM – IV is essential.*
  - **Second-line:** Water-soluble contrast enema (for low rectal/colonic leaks) – but CT is faster and more comprehensive.
  - **Limited utility:** Abdominal XR – looking for free air (but post-op air is common); may show extraluminal contrast in selected cases.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Drain fluid biochemistry**: amylase >3x serum suggests upper GI leak; bilirubin > serum suggests biliary leak.
  - Diagnostic aspirate / peritoneal tap (if CT equivocal and high suspicion – rarely done, usually surgical decision).
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **No single validated scoring system for leak.** Clinical judgement + elevated CRP + tachycardia (E.g. **CRP >150 mg/L** on POD 3–5 has high sensitivity for leak – but not specific).
- **Never-miss test:** **CT abdomen with IV contrast.** Delay or omission is a serious error – if stable enough, get it. If unstable, escalate before scanning.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ward/ED):**
  - **ABC + sepsis resuscitation** – high-flow O₂, IV access (wide bore), fluid bolus (Hartmann’s 500 mL stat, repeat as per BP/HR/lactate).
  - **Call surgical reg immediately** – do not wait for all results.
  - **NBM** – stop all oral/NG feeds.
  - **NG tube** if not already in situ – free drainage, low suction.
  - **Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics** (see Drugs section) – give first dose within 1 hour of suspicion.
  - **Blood cultures** before antibiotics (if delay minimal).
  - **Analgesia** – morphine IV (titrate) but avoid masking peritonism.
  - **Consider ICU referral** if NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >4, requiring vasopressors.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Surgery** – definitive management is re-laparotomy (or percutaneous drainage for contained abscess without free leak). *Do not delay for trial of medical therapy.*
  - **If not for surgery (e.g. poor candidate)**: percutaneous drainage of collections + antibiotics + supportive care (ICU).
  - **Monitor** – hourly observations (NEWS2), strict fluid balance, urine output, drain output.
  - **Organ support** – may need vasopressors (noradrenaline, under ICU guidance), renal replacement therapy.
  - **Nutrition** – parenteral nutrition (TPN) typically required post-operatively because bowel rest imperative.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Stoma formation often required at re-operation; may be temporary or permanent.
  - Wound care, VTE prophylaxis, physiotherapy.
  - Follow-up surgical clinic 4–6 weeks post-discharge; consider CT or contrast study before stoma reversal.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** Hartmann’s at maintenance + replacement (guided by UOP, losses). Avoid KCl until renal function known.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (check weight, CrCl). *Hold if active bleeding.*
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM completely. NG free drainage.
  - **Observations:** Minimum 4-hourly (NEWS2), more frequently if unstable: 1-hourly for first 24h.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line antibiotics:**
  - **Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV TDS** (check local policy for dose interval if renal impairment).
  - or **Cefuroxime 1.5 g IV TDS + Metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS** as alternative.
- **If penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis):**
  - **Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV BD** (avoid if high risk of MRSA resistance) + Metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS.
  - or **Gentamicin 5–7 mg/kg IV once daily** (check trough levels before second dose) + Metronidazole.
- **Analgesia:**
  - Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV PRN (titrate) or PCA if appropriate.
  - Paracetamol 1g IV QDS (prescribe regularly).
- **Fluid therapy:**
  - Hartmann’s at 1–2 mL/kg/hour maintenance. Boluses as per haemodynamics.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid NSAIDs (risk of acute kidney injury / bleed).
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: caution if CrCl <20; dose adjust.
  - Gentamicin: nephrotoxic, ototoxic; check levels – **trough <1 mg/L** (or <0.5 for once daily); avoid with other nephrotoxins.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Gentamicin** – not checking levels before second dose can cause permanent nephrotoxicity. **Never repeat without trough level.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Septic shock, multi-organ failure.
  - Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS).
  - Acute kidney injury (common, multifactorial).
  - Wound dehiscence / evisceration.
  - Enterocutaneous fistula (chronic, from persistent leak).
  - Intra-abdominal abscess (residual).
- **Chronic:**
  - Prolonged ileus, failure to thrive.
  - Stoma-related issues (if formed).
  - Incisional hernia.
  - Chronic pain / adhesive small bowel obstruction.

### Prognosis
- 30-day mortality ~10–25% (up to 50% if delayed recognition).
- Recovery may require weeks in ICU; many survive with stoma, prolonged TPN.
- Early recognition (within 12–24h of onset) dramatically improves survival.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always review drain fluid colour every shift** – faeculent or bilious is a red flag.
- **Check CRP trajectory daily** – a rise on POD 3–4 is more specific than absolute value.
- **Instruct nurses to test drain fluid with bedside amylase strip** – if positive, escalate rapidly.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not attribute tachycardia to “post-op pain”** – it is a cardinal sign of leak until proven otherwise.
- **Do not stop antibiotics after 24h if no positive cultures** – treat empirically for full course until leak is controlled.
- **Do not wait for “classic” peritonitis** – in early leak, signs may be absent; go with gut feeling + labs.
- **Do not delay CT for oral contrast** – IV contrast is the priority; oral may slow the scan.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** abdominal pain worsens, fever >38°C, vomiting, inability to pass stool/flatus, drain changes colour or starts draining bloody/faeculent fluid.
- **Follow-up:** Surgical clinic in 2 weeks. Advise to contact hospital if any concern (provide dedicated ward or on-call number).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call surgical reg immediately if:** suspicion of leak (NEWS2 ≥5, any sign of peritonitis, haemodynamic instability, lactate >2 mmol/L rising).
- **Call ICU if:** lactate >4 mmol/L, persistent hypotension after 2 L fluid (MAP <65), NEWS2 ≥7, requiring vasopressors, oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/h despite adequate filling.
- **Call crash team if:** unresponsive, peri-arrest (e.g. GCS <8, respiratory rate <8 or >30, systolic BP <90 unresponsive to fluids).
